question: does mycorrhizal fungi really work
title: Does Mycorrhizal Fungi Really Work? Yes—and Here's Why Your Fertilizer Is Getting in the Way
metaDescription: Mycorrhizal fungi form symbiotic networks that deliver nutrients to plants in exchange for carbon. The science is clear. Scotty explains what it does, why you're probably disrupting it, and how to let it work.
content:
# Does Mycorrhizal Fungi Really Work?

Y'all, I get a little bit fired up about this topic. Not because mycorrhizal fungi is a hard subject — it's actually pretty simple once you understand the basics. I get fired up because once you understand what this network does for your plants, and then you look at what conventional gardening and farming practices do to it, you realize we've been actively destroying one of the most powerful biological systems on the planet. For decades. On purpose. Because it was cheaper in the short run.

So let's talk about what mycorrhizal fungi actually is, whether it works, and what you can do about it.

## What Mycorrhizal Fungi Is and What It Does

Mycorrhiza is a relationship — a symbiotic partnership between fungi and plant roots. The word literally comes from the Greek for "fungus root." The fungi attach to or colonize the root system of a plant and extend filaments called hyphae out into the soil in every direction. These hyphae are vastly thinner than plant roots and can reach into tiny soil pores that roots cannot access.

Through this network, the fungi gather phosphorus, nitrogen, water, and trace minerals that the plant cannot reach on its own. The fungi deliver those nutrients directly to the root. In exchange, the plant feeds the fungi carbohydrates — sugar made through photosynthesis. The plant provides carbon, the fungi provides nutrients and water access. Both parties benefit.

This relationship exists in an estimated 80 to 90 percent of all land plants. It is not a specialty adaptation. It is the normal, default condition of plant life on earth. Plants evolved alongside mycorrhizal networks. This partnership is hundreds of millions of years old.

## Does the Science Say It Works?

Yes. Clearly, unambiguously, with a substantial body of peer-reviewed research supporting it.

Arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi (AMF) — the most common type in vegetable gardens — have been shown to significantly improve plant growth, particularly in poor or depleted soils. Plants grown with healthy mycorrhizal associations are routinely larger and more productive than the same plants grown in the same soil without those associations. The effect is strongest in low-fertility conditions, which makes sense: when nutrients are scarce, the fungi's ability to reach otherwise inaccessible mineral pockets becomes even more valuable.

Beyond growth, AMF improve soil structure by producing a protein called glomalin that binds soil particles into stable aggregates. Good soil structure means better water retention, better drainage, better aeration — everything a productive garden soil needs. The fungal hyphae also sequester carbon in the soil and support the entire microbial community living in and around the root zone.

Gabe Brown talks about this in *Dirt to Soil* when he describes watching mycorrhizal networks develop on his ranch as he transitioned away from tillage and synthetic inputs. The soil changed visibly over years. The structure improved. The biology came back. With it, the productivity came back — without the chemical inputs that used to be required to maintain it.

## Why Your Fertilizer Is Undermining the Whole Thing

When you add readily available phosphorus and nitrogen to your soil — the kind that comes in a bag or a jug — you short-circuit the mycorrhizal deal.

The plant doesn't need the fungi anymore. Everything it needs is sitting right there in the soil, dissolved and ready to be absorbed. So the plant stops feeding the fungi. The carbohydrate flow shuts down. The fungal network shrinks or dies back. Over time, without that network, the soil structure degrades, water retention declines, and nutrient cycling slows down. You need more fertilizer to make up for the biology you just killed.

When you add synthetic fertilizers to the ground, you damage the microbial and fungal communities that exist there. The plant stops sending carbon to the fungi because there's no need for the exchange. The whole biological network unravels one fertilizer application at a time.

This is the treadmill that conventional agriculture is on. You fertilize to grow crops, the fertilizer kills the biology, the dead biology means the soil can hold less fertility on its own, so you fertilize more. Every generation of that cycle leaves the soil more dependent on external inputs and less capable of supporting itself.

## The Tillage Problem

Synthetic fertilizer is not the only way to destroy mycorrhizal networks. Tillage does it just as efficiently. The hyphal networks that fungi build through soil are physical structures — actual threads running through the soil profile. A tiller shreds them. One pass through the garden with a rototiller can destroy years of fungal network development in minutes.

This is one of the core arguments for no-till gardening and farming. Not just that tillage compacts the soil and destroys structure, but that it physically destroys the biological network that makes the soil productive. Every time you till, you are starting over from scratch biologically. The plants have to rebuild those partnerships from zero.

I went no-till in my raised beds years ago. No pitching, no turning over. I apply compost to the top and let the biology move it down through the profile. It's slower at first, but the biology that builds up is real and durable and it does work that no synthetic input can replicate.

## How to Let Mycorrhizal Fungi Work for You

The approach is simpler than people expect. Stop killing it. That's most of the job.

Stop tilling. Apply compost and organic mulch at the surface and let soil organisms incorporate it. Reduce or eliminate synthetic fertilizers, especially phosphorus, which is the primary trigger for the plant to disengage from the fungal partnership. Keep living roots in the soil as much of the year as possible — cover crops between seasons, perennials where practical. Living roots feed the fungi. Bare ground starves them.

If you're starting with severely depleted soil — which most urban gardeners are — you can also inoculate with commercial mycorrhizal products. These introduce spores that, given the right conditions, will establish networks fairly quickly. The key phrase there is "given the right conditions." If you inoculate and then add synthetic fertilizer, you've wasted your money.

This is a little bit of a commitment, I won't lie. But once the biology establishes itself, it becomes self-sustaining. The garden starts working harder for you every year. That's what regenerative means. Not just neutral. Actually getting better over time.

## Sources

- [Frontiers in Plant Science: Role of Arbuscular Mycorrhizal Fungi in Plant Growth Regulation](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2019.01068/full)
- [PMC: Mycorrhiza: a natural resource assists plant growth under varied soil conditions](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7165205/)
- [PMC: Roles of Arbuscular Mycorrhizal Fungi on Soil Fertility](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10512336/)
- [Science: How mycorrhizal associations drive plant population and community biology](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.aba1223)
- [PMC: Symbiotic synergy: How Arbuscular Mycorrhizal Fungi enhance nutrient uptake, stress tolerance, and soil health](https://imafungus.pensoft.net/article/144989/)
